title.zh: 卷六十七 楊胡朱梅云傳
title.en: Volume 67: Yang, Hu, Zhu, Mei and Yun
title.raw: 卷六十七 楊胡朱梅云傳 Volume 67: Yang, Hu, Zhu, Mei and Yun

sentences.zh: 卷六十七·楊胡朱梅云傳第三十七

translations.literal: Volume 67: Biographies of Yang, Hu, Zhu, Mei, and Yun—the thirty-seventh.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 67: Biographies of Yang, Hu, Zhu, Mei, and Yun—the thirty-seventh series.

sentences.zh: 楊王孫

translations.literal: Yang Wangsun.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Wangsun

sentences.zh: 楊王孫者，孝武時人也。

translations.literal: Yang Wangsun was a man of Emperor Xiaowu's time.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Wangsun lived under Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 學黃老之術，家業千金，厚自奉養生，亡所不致。

translations.literal: He studied the arts of Huang and Lao; his household estate was worth a thousand in gold; he richly provided for himself in nurturing life—nothing was left untried.
translations.idiomatic: He studied Huang-Lao, commanded a fortune in gold, and pursued longevity with every luxury money could buy.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「致，至也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Zhi means 'to reach.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses the word zhi (here "bring to bear") as "reach the utmost."

sentences.zh: 〉及病且終，先令其子，〈師古曰：「先令，爲遺令。」

translations.literal: 〉When his illness was nearing its end, he first gave orders to his son. 〈Yan's note: "First orders means a testamentary instruction."〉
translations.idiomatic: As death neared he issued final instructions to his son—what commentators call a "first command," meaning a deathbed will.

sentences.zh: 〉曰：「吾欲臝葬，以反吾真，〈師古曰：「臝者，不爲衣衾棺槨者也。

translations.literal: 〉He said, "I wish to be buried naked, to return to my authenticity. 〈Yan's note: "Naked burial means without grave-clothes, inner or outer coffin."
translations.idiomatic: He said, "I want a naked burial—to go back to what is real. Yan Shigu: naked burial omits shroud, inner coffin, and outer shell."

sentences.zh: 反，歸也。

translations.literal: Fan means 'return.'
translations.idiomatic: "Return" means to go back.

sentences.zh: 真者，自然之道也。

translations.literal: The authentic is the way of spontaneity.
translations.idiomatic: The real is nature's own course.

sentences.zh: 臝音郎果反。」

translations.literal: Naked is read lang-guo fan."
translations.idiomatic: The word for naked burial is pronounced like the syllables lang-guo in the fanqie spelling (ancient reading guide)."

sentences.zh: 〉必亡易吾意。

translations.literal: 〉You must not alter my intent.
translations.idiomatic: Do not change my mind.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「易，改也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yi means 'to change.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses yi as "alter" or "change."

sentences.zh: 〉死則爲布囊盛尸，入地七尺，旣下，從足引脫其囊，以身親土。」

translations.literal: 〉When I die, wrap the corpse in a cloth bag, put it in the earth seven feet deep; once it is down, pull the bag off from the feet and let the body touch the soil directly."
translations.idiomatic: When I die, sew me in a sack, lower me seven feet, then strip the sack upward from my feet until my flesh meets native earth."

sentences.zh: 其子欲默而不從，重廢父命，〈師古曰：「重，難也。」

translations.literal: His son wished to be silent and not obey, but found it hard to set aside his father's command. 〈Yan's note: "Zhong means 'difficult.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: The son wanted to refuse yet shrank from disobeying a father—zhong meaning "a hard choice."

sentences.zh: 〉欲從之，心又不忍，迺往見王孫友人祁侯。

translations.literal: He wished to obey, yet his heart could not bear it; thereupon he went to see Wangsun's friend Marquis of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: If he obeyed, his heart rebelled; so he went to Wangsun's friend, the Marquis of Qi.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「祁侯繒賀之孫承嗣者，名它。」〉

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Marquis of Qi is the heir who continued the line of Zeng He, named Ta."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: the Marquis of Qi was Zeng He's grandson-heir, named Ta.

sentences.zh: 祁侯與王孫書曰：「王孫苦疾，僕迫從上祠雍，未得詣前。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Qi wrote to Wangsun: "Wangsun, you suffer gravely in illness; your servant was pressed to follow the Sovereign to sacrifice at Yong and could not come to your bedside.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Qi wrote: "Friend, your illness is cruel; I have been tied to the emperor's Yong sacrifice and could not visit you."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「詣，至也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yi means 'to arrive.'
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses yi as "to arrive" or "reach."

sentences.zh: 至前，言來見也。」

translations.literal: Arriving before you means coming to see you."〉
translations.idiomatic: To come before you is to pay a visit."

sentences.zh: 〉願存精神，省思慮，進醫藥，厚自持。

translations.literal: I hope you will preserve your spirit, reduce anxious thought, take medicine, and strongly sustain yourself.
translations.idiomatic: Guard your strength, worry less, take physic, and be kind to yourself.

sentences.zh: 竊聞王孫先令臝葬，令死者亡知則已，若其有知，是戮尸地下，將臝見先人，竊爲王孫不取也。

translations.literal: I have privately heard of your testament ordering naked burial: if the dead have no awareness, then it ends there; if they have awareness, this is exposing the corpse to mutilation underground; you would meet your forebears naked—I privately think this unworthy for you.
translations.idiomatic: Your naked-burial will: if the dead feel nothing, very well; if they do, you invite the shame of a corpse abused in the dark and would greet ancestors stripped—surely that cannot be your wish.

sentences.zh: 且孝經曰『爲之棺椁衣衾』，是亦聖人之遺制，何必區區獨守所聞？

translations.literal: Moreover the Classic of Filial Piety says 'for them make inner and outer coffin and grave-clothes'—that too is the surviving regulation of the sages; why must you narrowly cling alone to what you have heard?
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Filial Piety still commands coffin, shell, and winding-sheet—the sages' own rule. Why hug one eccentric opinion?

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「區區，小意也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Ququ means petty intent."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: ququ means petty, narrow-minded fussing.

sentences.zh: 〉願王孫察焉。」

translations.literal: I hope you will examine this."
translations.idiomatic: Think again, I beg you."

sentences.zh: 王孫報曰：「蓋聞古之聖王，緣人情不忍其親，故爲制禮，今則越之，〈師古曰：「言踰禮而厚葬也。」

translations.literal: Wangsun replied: "I have heard that the sage kings of antiquity, following human feeling and unable to bear [separation from] their kin, therefore instituted ritual; now people leap over it. 〈Yan's note: "It means they exceed ritual with lavish burial."〉
translations.idiomatic: Wangsun answered: "The ancient kings framed rites from human pity for the dead; today men vault past those bounds into wasteful tombs. Yan Shigu notes: they overspend the rites."

sentences.zh: 〉吾是以臝葬，將以矯世也。

translations.literal: Therefore I use naked burial—I will thereby correct the age.
translations.idiomatic: I choose naked burial to jerk the age back toward sanity.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「正曲曰矯。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "To straighten the crooked is jiao."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: jiao means to straighten what is bent.

sentences.zh: 〉夫厚葬誠亡益於死者，而俗人競以相高，靡財單幣，腐之地下。

translations.literal: Now lavish burial truly brings no benefit to the dead, yet vulgar men vie to outdo one another in it, scattering wealth and exhausting treasure to rot it underground.
translations.idiomatic: Rich tombs help the corpse not at all, yet the world competes in piling treasure underground until fortunes dissolve.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「靡，散也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Mi means 'to scatter.'
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses mi as "scatter" or "squander."

sentences.zh: 單，盡也。」

translations.literal: Dan means 'to exhaust.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Dan means "drain dry."

sentences.zh: 〉或迺今日入而明日發，〈師古曰：「言見發掘也。」

translations.literal: Some are interred today and exhumed tomorrow. 〈Yan's note: "It means they are dug up by robbers."〉
translations.idiomatic: Some are sealed tonight and rifled tomorrow—Yan Shigu: opened by thieves.

sentences.zh: 〉此真與暴骸於中野何異！

translations.literal: How is this different from exposing bones in the open field!
translations.idiomatic: That is no better than leaving carrion on the plain.

sentences.zh: 且夫死者，終生之化，而物之歸者也。

translations.literal: Moreover the dead are the transformation that ends life and the return to which things go.
translations.idiomatic: The dead are life's last change, the homecoming all matter makes.

sentences.zh: 歸者得至，化者得變，是物各反其真也。

translations.literal: What returns can reach its end; what transforms can change—thus each thing turns back to its authenticity.
translations.idiomatic: Return finishes the journey; change sheds the old—each creature goes home to its own nature.

sentences.zh: 反真冥冥，亡形亡聲，迺合道情。

translations.literal: Returning to authenticity is dark and silent, without form or sound—then it matches the disposition of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: In that dark return, shape and voice gone, the man at last fits the Way.

sentences.zh: 夫飾外以華衆，厚葬以鬲真，〈師古曰：「鬲與隔同。

translations.literal: To adorn the outside to dazzle the crowd, to bury thickly and thereby block the authentic—〈Yan's note: "Ge is the same as ge 'to block.'
translations.idiomatic: Gaudy display for the living and thick coffins for the dead both wall off the true return. Yan Shigu reads the character here as "block" or "separate off."

sentences.zh: 其後並類此。」

translations.literal: What follows is all of this kind."〉
translations.idiomatic: The glosses that follow run on the same principle."

sentences.zh: 〉使歸者不得至，化者不得變，是使物各失其所也。

translations.literal: It makes what should return unable to reach its end, what should transform unable to change—thus each thing loses its proper station.
translations.idiomatic: You trap the traveler short of the inn and freeze the chrysalis in the shell—every creature misses its appointed end.

sentences.zh: 且吾聞之，精神者天之有也，形骸者地之有也。

translations.literal: Moreover I have heard that the vital spirit belongs to what Heaven has, the bodily frame to what Earth has.
translations.idiomatic: The hun soul is heaven's loan; the bones are earth's clay.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「文子稱天氣爲魂。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "The Wenzi calls heaven's breath the hun.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu cites the Wenzi: celestial breath is the ethereal soul."

sentences.zh: 延陵季子云『骨肉下歸於土』，是以云然。」

translations.literal: Jizha of Yanling said, 'Flesh and bone sink down to earth'—therefore it is said so."〉
translations.idiomatic: Jizha of Yanling said, "Flesh and bone return to mud"—the same teaching."

sentences.zh: 〉精神離形，各歸其真，故謂之鬼，鬼之爲言歸也。

translations.literal: The spirit leaves the form; each returns to its authenticity—therefore it is called a gui; speaking of gui means 'return.'
translations.idiomatic: Spirit and carcass part, each to its element—hence we call the dead gui, "those who have gone home."

sentences.zh: 其尸塊然獨處，〈師古曰：「塊音口對反。」

translations.literal: The corpse sits there lumpish and alone. 〈Yan's note: "Kuai is read kou-dui fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: The husk sits lumpish and alone; Yan Shigu gives the fanqie spelling (kou-dui) for the word rendered "lumpish."

sentences.zh: 〉豈有知哉？

translations.literal: How could it have awareness?
translations.idiomatic: What mind is left in meat?

sentences.zh: 裹以幣帛，鬲以棺椁，支體絡束，口含玉石，欲化不得，鬱爲枯腊，千載之後，棺椁朽腐，迺得歸土，就其真宅。

translations.literal: Wrapped in silks, blocked by inner and outer coffins, limbs trussed, mouth filled with jade and stone—it cannot transform but stagnates into dry flesh; after a thousand years, when coffin shells rot, only then can it return to earth and reach its true dwelling.
translations.idiomatic: Swaddle it in brocade, box it in double caskets, bind the limbs, plug the mouth with jade—you forbid decay until it mummifies; only when wood rots a thousand years later does dust rejoin dust.

sentences.zh: 繇是言之，焉用乆客！

translations.literal: From this speaking, what use is long lodging as a guest!
translations.idiomatic: Why prolong a guest lease on nothing?

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「言不用乆爲客也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "It means do not long remain a guest.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: do not overstay as a traveler in the world."

sentences.zh: 繇讀與由同。」

translations.literal: You is read the same as you."〉
translations.idiomatic: The graph for you ("thereby") is read like the homophone you meaning "from" or "by."

sentences.zh: 〉昔帝堯之葬也，窾木爲匵，葛藟爲緘，〈服虔曰：「窾音款。

translations.literal: In antiquity, when Emperor Yao was buried, they hollowed wood for a chest and used creeper of dolichos as ties. 〈Fu Qian's note: "Kuan is read kuan; kuan means 'hollow'; hollow wood for a chest."〉
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yao's grave was a hollowed log for a coffin, wild grapevine for straps—Fu Qian says the first word is read kuan, meaning "hollowed."

sentences.zh: 款，空也，空木爲匵。」

translations.literal: Kuan means empty; empty wood for a chest."
translations.idiomatic: Empty timber formed the shell."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「匵即櫝字也。

translations.literal: Yan's note: "Du is the same character as du 'chest.'
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: the graph here is the same as the word for a small chest or inner coffin.

sentences.zh: 櫝，小棺也。

translations.literal: Du means a small coffin.
translations.idiomatic: A small inner box.

sentences.zh: 藟，葛蔓也。

translations.literal: Lei means creeper of dolichos.
translations.idiomatic: Lei is dolichos vine.

sentences.zh: 一曰，藟亦草名，葛之類也。

translations.literal: One opinion says lei is also a plant name, a kind of dolichos.
translations.idiomatic: Another gloss: lei names a vine like kudzu.

sentences.zh: 緘，束也。

translations.literal: Jian means to bind.
translations.idiomatic: Jian means lash tight.

sentences.zh: 藟音力水反。

translations.literal: Lei is read li-shui fan.
translations.idiomatic: The plant name lei is spelled in the fanqie system as li-shui.

sentences.zh: 緘音工咸反。」

translations.literal: Jian is read gong-xian fan.""
translations.idiomatic: The word jian (to bind) is spelled in fanqie as gong-xian."

sentences.zh: 〉其穿下不亂泉，〈師古曰：「亂，絕也。」

translations.literal: 〉The shaft did not cut off the spring below, 〈Yan's note: "Luan means 'to cut off.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: The pit did not pierce the aquifer below—Yan Shigu reads the verb as "cut off" the spring.

sentences.zh: 〉上不泄殠。

translations.literal: Above it did not vent stench to the sky.
translations.idiomatic: Nor did stench seep upward to offend heaven.

sentences.zh: 故聖王生易尚，死易葬也。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage kings found life easy to honor and death easy to bury.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the sage kings lived simply and buried simply.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「尚，崇也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Shang means 'to esteem.'
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses shang as "honor" or "esteem."

sentences.zh: 言生死皆儉約也。」

translations.literal: It means both life and death were frugal."〉
translations.idiomatic: Both ends of life stayed spare."

sentences.zh: 〉不加功於亡用，不損財於亡謂。

translations.literal: They did not add labor to the useless, did not waste wealth on meaningless display.
translations.idiomatic: They wasted no labor on the pointless, no coin on empty show.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「謂者，名稱也，亦指趣也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Wei means name, also meaning aim or tendency."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: wei means "name" or "pretext."

sentences.zh: 〉今費財厚葬，留歸鬲至，死者不知，生者不得，是謂重惑。

translations.literal: Now to squander wealth on thick burial, to detain the return and block arrival—the dead know nothing of it, the living gain nothing from it: this is called double delusion.
translations.idiomatic: Today men bankrupt kin for tombs that neither comfort the dead nor enrich the living—twice deluded.

sentences.zh: 於戲！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 吾不爲也。」

translations.literal: I will not do it."
translations.idiomatic: I will not do it."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「於讀曰烏。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yu is read wu.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: the exclamation yu is read like wu."

sentences.zh: 戲讀曰呼。」〉

translations.literal: Xi is read hu."〉
translations.idiomatic: The second syllable xi is read like hu (a sigh)."

sentences.zh: 祁侯曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The Marquis of Qi said, "Excellent."
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Qi said, "Well argued."

sentences.zh: 遂臝葬。

translations.literal: Thereupon he was buried naked.
translations.idiomatic: They buried him as he asked—stripped to earth.

sentences.zh: 胡建字子孟，河東人也。

translations.literal: Hu Jian, courtesy name Zimeng, was a man of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Jian, courtesy Zimeng, came from Hedong.

sentences.zh: 孝武天漢中，守軍正丞，〈師古曰：「南北軍各有正，正又置丞，而建未得真官，兼守之。」

translations.literal: In Emperor Wu's Tianhan era he acted as assistant to the army's chief rectifier. 〈Yan's note: "North and south armies each had a chief rectifier, who again had an assistant; Jian had not yet obtained substantive office but was holding the post concurrently."〉
translations.idiomatic: Under Wu's Tianhan reign he served as acting deputy to the chief army judge—Yan Shigu: each host had a rectifier and deputy; Jian held the post without full rank.

sentences.zh: 〉貧亡車馬，常步與走卒起居，所以尉薦走卒，甚得其心。

translations.literal: He was too poor for carriage or horse and always walked, sharing rise and rest with the runners, whereby he comforted and advanced the runners—he won their hearts extremely.
translations.idiomatic: Too poor for a cart, he marched with the camp runners, sharing their bed and board until they loved him.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「尉者，自上安之也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Wei means to soothe from above.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: wei here means to reassure or soothe from above."

sentences.zh: 薦者，舉籍也。」

translations.literal: Jian means to raise on the roster."〉
translations.idiomatic: Jian in this sense means to enroll someone on the promotion roster."

sentences.zh: 〉時監軍御史爲姦，穿北軍壘垣以爲賈區，〈師古曰：「坐賣曰賈，爲賣物之區也。

translations.literal: At that time the supervising army Imperial Clerk did wrong, breaching the north army's rampart to make a market stall. 〈Yan's note: "Sitting sale is gu; it means a place for selling goods.
translations.idiomatic: The supervising censor broke the camp palisade to open a shop—Yan Shigu explains the word for merchant stall as a seated market, a place for selling goods."

sentences.zh: 區者，小室之名，若今小庵屋之類耳。

translations.literal: Qu means the name of a small chamber, like today's little hut.
translations.idiomatic: Qu names a small booth, like a guard hut.

sentences.zh: 故衞士之屋謂之區廬，宿衞宮外士稱爲區士也。

translations.literal: Therefore the guards' quarters are called qu-lu, and the guards who lodge outside the palace are called qu-shi.
translations.idiomatic: Hence guard sheds are called qu-lu, and exterior palace sentries are called qu-shi.

sentences.zh: 賈音古。

translations.literal: Gu is read gu.
translations.idiomatic: The merchant graph is read with the same sound as the word for "merchant" (gu).

sentences.zh: 其下亦同。」

translations.literal: What follows below is the same."〉
translations.idiomatic: The same reading applies below."

sentences.zh: 〉建欲誅之，迺約其走卒曰：〈師古曰：「約，束也。」

translations.literal: Jian wished to execute him; thereupon he bound his runners with agreement. 〈Yan's note: "Yue means 'to bind.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Jian bound his runners with an oath—Yan Shigu glosses yue as "bind" or "pledge."

sentences.zh: 〉「我欲與公有所誅，吾言取之則取，斬之則斬。」

translations.literal: "I wish with you gentlemen to execute someone: when I say 'take him,' take; when I say 'strike him off,' strike him off."
translations.idiomatic: "We are going to kill a man: when I say take, you seize; when I say strike, you strike."

sentences.zh: 於是當選士馬日，監御史與護軍諸校列坐堂皇上，〈師古曰：「校者，軍之諸部校也。

translations.literal: Therefore on the day when picked troops and horses were reviewed, the supervising clerk and the protectors' various colonels sat in rows in the open hall. 〈Yan's note: "Colonel means the army's various colonels' sections.
translations.idiomatic: On muster day the censor and the colonels sat in the roofless hall—Yan Shigu identifies jiao (regimental commands) as the army's company sections."

sentences.zh: 室無四壁曰皇。」

translations.literal: A chamber without four walls is called huang."〉
translations.idiomatic: Huang names an open hall without four enclosing walls."

sentences.zh: 〉建從走卒趨至堂皇下拜謁，因上堂皇，走卒皆上，建指監御史曰：「取彼。」

translations.literal: Jian came from the runners at a run to the foot of the hall, bowed in audience, then ascended the hall; the runners all ascended; Jian pointed at the supervising clerk and said, "Take that man."
translations.idiomatic: Jian rushed up with the runners, bowed, mounted the platform, and pointed: "Seize him."

sentences.zh: 走卒前曳下堂皇。

translations.literal: The runners came forward and dragged him down from the hall.
translations.idiomatic: They dragged the censor from the dais.

sentences.zh: 建曰：「斬之。」

translations.literal: Jian said, "Strike him off."
translations.idiomatic: "Behead him."

sentences.zh: 遂斬御史。

translations.literal: Thereupon they beheaded the clerk.
translations.idiomatic: The blade fell.

sentences.zh: 護軍諸校皆愕驚，不知所以。

translations.literal: The protectors' colonels were all stunned and alarmed, not knowing why.
translations.idiomatic: The colonels sat frozen, not grasping what had happened.

sentences.zh: 建亦已有成奏在其懷中，遂上奏曰：「臣聞軍法，立武以威衆，誅惡以禁邪。

translations.literal: Jian already had a completed memorial in his bosom; thereupon he submitted a memorial saying, "Your servant has heard that in military law one establishes martial awesomeness to overawe the masses and punishes evil to forbid deviance.
translations.idiomatic: Jian drew from his breast a finished memorial: "The code says awe checks the ranks and punishment bars crime."

sentences.zh: 今監御史公穿軍垣以求賈利，〈師古曰：「公謂顯然爲之。」

translations.literal: Now the supervising clerk has publicly breached the army wall to seek merchant profit. 〈Yan's note: "Gong means he did it openly."〉
translations.idiomatic: This censor openly breached the camp wall for trade—Yan Shigu reads gong as "openly" or "in public view."

sentences.zh: 〉私買賣以與士巿，不立剛毅之心，勇猛之節，亡以帥先士大夫，尤失理不公。

translations.literal: Privately buying and selling to market with the soldiers—he does not set up a heart of firm sternness or a quality of fierce courage; he has no means to lead and take the lead of great officers and gentlemen—this is especially losing principle and lacking fairness.
translations.idiomatic: He peddled inside the lines, showed no stern courage, and could not lead officers—gross injustice by statute.

sentences.zh: 用文吏議，不至重法。

translations.literal: If judged by clerical deliberation, it would not reach heavy penalty.
translations.idiomatic: A civil tribunal might call it a light offense.

sentences.zh: 黃帝李法曰：〈蘇林曰：「獄官名也。

translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor's Li Code says: 〈Su Lin's note: "It is the name of a prison official.
translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor's Li canons—Su Lin: Li was a judicial title."

sentences.zh: 天文志『左角李，右角將』。」

translations.literal: The Treatise on Astronomy says 'left horn Li, right horn general.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: The monograph on astronomy pairs the left horn with Li."

sentences.zh: 孟康曰：「兵書之法也。」

translations.literal: Meng Kang's note: "It is the method in military books.""
translations.idiomatic: Meng Kang: a military-law formula."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「李者，法官之號也，緫主征伐刑戮之事也，故稱其書曰李法。

translations.literal: Yan's note: "Li is the title of a judge; he overall masters punitive expeditions and executions; therefore their book is called the Li Code.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: Li judges war and punishment, hence the Li Code—Su Lin's gloss is closest."

sentences.zh: 蘇說近之。」

translations.literal: Su's explanation is near the mark."〉
translations.idiomatic: Su Lin had the sense of it."

sentences.zh: 〉『壁壘已定，穿窬不繇路，是謂姦人，姦人者殺。』

translations.literal: 'When ramparts and palisades are already fixed, to bore a thief-hole not by the road—this is called a traitor; traitors are killed.'"
translations.idiomatic: "Once walls stand, tunneling off the line is treason—treason earns death."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「窬，小竇也，音踰。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yu means a small hole, read yu."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains the word for a thief-tunnel as a small hole, with the reading given in fanqie spelling.

sentences.zh: 繇讀與由同。

translations.literal: You is read the same as you.
translations.idiomatic: The graph is read like the homophone meaning "from" or "thereby."

sentences.zh: 下皆類此。」

translations.literal: What follows below is all of this kind."
translations.idiomatic: The same glossing convention applies below."

sentences.zh: 〉臣謹桉軍法曰：『正亡屬將軍，將軍有罪以聞，〈師古曰：「言軍正不屬將軍。

translations.literal: 〉Your servant respectfully according to military law says: 'The chief rectifier does not belong to the general; if the general has a crime, one may report it to be heard. 〈Yan's note: "It means the army rectifier does not belong to the general."
translations.idiomatic: Your servant cites the code: the chief judge answers to the throne, not the camp commander; a general's crimes go up by memorial. Yan Shigu stresses that the rectifier is not the general's subordinate."

sentences.zh: 將軍有罪過，得表奏之。」

translations.literal: If the general has faults, one may memorialize them."〉
translations.idiomatic: Any fault on the general's part may be tabled for the emperor."

sentences.zh: 〉二千石以下行法焉。』

translations.literal: 'Those of two thousand piculs and below carry out the law.'"
translations.idiomatic: Officials at two thousand piculs and below enforce the statute."

sentences.zh: 〈孟康曰：「二千石謂軍中校尉、都尉之屬。」

translations.literal: 〈Meng Kang's note: "Two thousand piculs means colonels and chief commandants in the army."〉
translations.idiomatic: Meng Kang clarifies: here "two thousand piculs" covers colonels and chief commandants.

sentences.zh: 〉丞於用法疑，〈孟康曰：「丞屬軍正，斬御史於法有疑。」

translations.literal: 〉As assistant I had doubt in applying the law. 〈Meng Kang's note: "The assistant belongs to the chief rectifier; beheading the clerk was doubtful in law."〉
translations.idiomatic: As deputy I hesitated at the law's edge—Meng Kang notes the deputy served the chief rectifier, so killing a censor sat in a gray zone.

sentences.zh: 〉執事不諉上，〈師古曰：「諉，累也。

translations.literal: 〉One who executes affairs does not shirk onto superiors. 〈Yan's note: "Wei means 'to burden.'
translations.idiomatic: The officer on the spot must act when the code demands—not pass the buck upward. Yan Shigu glosses wei as "burden" or "implicate."

sentences.zh: 言執事者，當見法即行，不可以事累於上也。

translations.literal: The meaning is that executors, when they see the law, should act immediately; they may not burden superiors with the affair.
translations.idiomatic: Execute the law at once; do not drag the throne into it."

sentences.zh: 諉音女瑞反。

translations.literal: Wei is read nu-rui fan.
translations.idiomatic: The character wei is spelled fanqie as nu-rui.

sentences.zh: 累音力瑞反。」

translations.literal: Lei is read li-rui fan.""
translations.idiomatic: The character lei is spelled fanqie as li-rui."

sentences.zh: 〉臣謹以斬，昧死以聞。」

translations.literal: 〉Your servant respectfully therefore struck him off, risking death to report."
translations.idiomatic: Your servant therefore took his head and reports at peril of his life."

sentences.zh: 制曰：「司馬法曰『國容不入軍，軍容不入國』，何文吏也？

translations.literal: Imperial rescript: "The Sima Fa says 'state deportment does not enter the army; army deportment does not enter the state'—why [cite] civil clerks?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered: "The Sima canons say camp law is not capital etiquette—why drag in civil judges?"

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「司馬法亦兵書之名也，解在主父偃傳。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Sima Fa is also the name of a military book; the explanation is in the biography of Zhufu Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: Sima Fa is a military treatise, as explained under Zhufu Yan."

sentences.zh: 詔言在於軍中，何用文吏議也。」

translations.literal: The edict's meaning is that it lies within the army—why use civil clerk deliberation?"〉
translations.idiomatic: The rescript puts the case under field law, not the ministry clerks."

sentences.zh: 〉三王或誓於軍中，欲民先成其慮也；

translations.literal: The Three Kings sometimes swore oaths in the army, wishing the people first to complete their planning;
translations.idiomatic: The three sage kings swore inside the camp so every soldier fixed his plan before battle;

sentences.zh: 或誓於軍門之外，欲民先意以待事也；

translations.literal: or swore outside the army gate, wishing the people first to set their intent while awaiting affairs;
translations.idiomatic: or they swore beyond the gate so the host steeled its will before the order came;

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「慮謂計念也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Lu means calculation and thought.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: lu is forethought, calculation."

sentences.zh: 先意謂先爲之意也。」

translations.literal: Xian yi means first to establish intent."〉
translations.idiomatic: Xian yi means shaping resolve beforehand."

sentences.zh: 〉或將交刃而誓，致民志也。』

translations.literal: or when about to cross blades they swore, fixing the people's will.'"
translations.idiomatic: Or they swore on the eve of steel to lock the army's courage."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「欲致民勇志，使不奔北。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Wishing to fix the people's brave will so they do not flee north."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: the oath was meant to nerve the ranks so none broke and ran."

sentences.zh: 〉建又何疑焉？」

translations.literal: What more doubt has Jian?"
translations.idiomatic: What doubt can Hu Jian still raise?"

sentences.zh: 建繇是顯名。

translations.literal: Jian thereby became famous.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Jian's name rang through the empire.

sentences.zh: 後爲渭城令，治甚有聲。

translations.literal: Later he was magistrate of Weicheng; his governance had great reputation.
translations.idiomatic: He later governed Weicheng county and earned a ringing reputation.

sentences.zh: 值昭帝幼，皇后父上官將軍安與帝姊蓋主私夫丁外人相善。

translations.literal: It happened that Emperor Zhao was young, and the empress's father, General Shangguan An, was intimate with the Princess of Gai's paramour Ding Wairen, the empress's elder sister's lover.
translations.idiomatic: Young Zhao sat the throne while the empress's father, General Shangguan An, consorted with Ding Wairen, lover of the emperor's sister the Princess of Gai.

sentences.zh: 外人驕恣，怨故京兆尹樊福，使客射殺之。

translations.literal: Wairen was arrogant and unrestrained; he resented the former capital governor Fan Fu and sent retainers to shoot and kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Wairen was insolent, hated the ex-governor Fan Fu, and had him shot by hired blades.

sentences.zh: 客臧公主廬，吏不敢捕。

translations.literal: The retainers hid in the princess's lodge, and the clerks dared not arrest them.
translations.idiomatic: The killers sheltered in the princess's compound; no constable dared touch them.

sentences.zh: 渭城令建將吏卒圍捕。

translations.literal: The magistrate of Weicheng, Jian, led clerks and soldiers to surround and capture them.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrate Hu Jian ringed the house with troops and moved to arrest.

sentences.zh: 蓋主聞之，與外人、上官將軍多從奴客往，犇射追吏，〈師古曰：「犇，古奔字也。

translations.literal: When the Princess of Gai heard it, she with Wairen and General Shangguan, with many slave-followers and retainers, went; they galloped and shot, pursuing the clerks. 〈Yan's note: "Ben is the ancient character for run."
translations.idiomatic: The princess rode out with Ding, Shangguan, and a mob of clients, galloping and shooting at Hu's men—Yan Shigu notes ben is the old graph for "run."

sentences.zh: 奔走赴之而射也。」

translations.literal: Running and hastening to them, they shot."〉
translations.idiomatic: They charged in shooting."

sentences.zh: 〉吏散走。

translations.literal: The clerks scattered and fled.
translations.idiomatic: Hu's detachment broke and ran.

sentences.zh: 主使僕射劾渭城令游徼傷主家奴。

translations.literal: The princess ordered her chief steward to impeach the Weicheng patrol officer for wounding her household slaves.
translations.idiomatic: She had her majordomo indict the county patrol for wounding her slaves.

sentences.zh: 建報亡它坐。

translations.literal: Jian reported that there was no other offense.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Jian answered that the patrol had committed no separate crime.

sentences.zh: 〈服虔曰：「言游徼奉公，無它坐也。」

translations.literal: 〈Fu Qian's note: "It means the patrol officer acted on public duty; there was no other offense."〉
translations.idiomatic: Fu Qian reads the line as praise of the patrol's lawful conduct.

sentences.zh: 〉蓋主怒，使人上書告建侵辱長公主，射甲舍門。

translations.literal: The Princess of Gai was angry and had someone memorialize accusing Jian of insulting a senior princess and shooting the gate of the armor hall.
translations.idiomatic: She struck back with a memorial accusing Hu Jian of lese-majeste and riddling the gate of her first-ranked mansion.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「甲舍即甲第，公主之宅。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Jia she is jia di, the princess's mansion."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: "armor hall" is another name for a nobleman's top-tier residence.

sentences.zh: 〉知吏賊傷奴，辟報故不窮審。

translations.literal: Knowing that clerks had wrongfully injured slaves, he gave a false return and therefore did not exhaustively investigate. 〈Su Lin's note: "Pi means to turn aside.
translations.idiomatic: Hu knew his men had bloodied her slaves yet filed a dodgy closing report and closed the case. Su Lin thought pi meant "turn aside."

sentences.zh: 〈蘇林曰：「辟，迴也。

translations.literal: Bao means verdict.
translations.idiomatic: Bao means "verdict" or "finding."

sentences.zh: 報，論也。

translations.literal: Deciding a prison case is bao.
translations.idiomatic: Closing a criminal file is called bao.

sentences.zh: 斷獄爲報。

translations.literal: Therefore the speech means there was a pretext.
translations.idiomatic: Su read it as "there was a stated reason."

sentences.zh: 故言有故也。

translations.literal: Not exhaustively investigating means not exhausting the affair."〉
translations.idiomatic: Not to investigate exhaustively is to leave the matter half done."

sentences.zh: 不窮審，不窮盡其事也。」

translations.literal: Yan's note: "Su's explanation is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu rejects Su Lin.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「蘇說非也。

translations.literal: It means that for the patrol officer he avoided guilt and falsely returned documents, and therefore was not exhaustively prosecuted."
translations.idiomatic: Hu means the patrol dodged blame with a sham report, so the case never went to trial."

sentences.zh: 言爲游徼避罪而妄報文書，故不窮治也。

translations.literal: Pi is read bi."〉
translations.idiomatic: The character pi is read like bi, "avoid."

sentences.zh: 辟讀曰避。」

translations.literal: Grand General Huo Guang shelved his memorial.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang buried the princess's bill.

sentences.zh: 〉大將軍霍光寢其奏。

translations.literal: Later when Guang fell ill, the Shangguan clan handled affairs in his stead; they sent subordinates to arrest Jian, and Jian committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: When Huo fell sick the Shangguans seized the reins, ordered Hu Jian arrested, and he killed himself.

sentences.zh: 後光病，上官氏代聽事，下吏捕建，建自殺。

translations.literal: Clerks and commoners called it injustice; to this day Weicheng has established his shrine.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Weicheng still call it murder and keep a shrine to his memory.

sentences.zh: 吏民稱冤，至今渭城立其祠。

translations.literal: Clerks and commoners called it injustice; to this day Weicheng has established his shrine.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Weicheng still call it murder and keep a shrine to his memory.

sentences.zh: 朱雲字游，魯人也，徙平陵。

translations.literal: Zhu Yun, courtesy name You, was a man of Lu who moved to Pingling.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun, courtesy You, was a Lu man who resettled at Pingling.

sentences.zh: 少時通輕俠，借客報仇。

translations.literal: In youth he consorted with knights-errant and light fellows, borrowing retainers to avenge grudges.
translations.idiomatic: As a youth he ran with swordsmen and hired blades for revenge.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「借，助也，音子夜反。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Jie means to assist, read zi-ye fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses jie as "lend aid," with fanqie spelling zi-ye.

sentences.zh: 〉長八尺餘，容貌甚壯，以勇力聞。

translations.literal: He was more than eight feet tall, of very martial appearance, and was famed for courage and strength.
translations.idiomatic: He stood over eight feet, looked every inch a fighter, and was known for raw strength.

sentences.zh: 年四十，迺變節從博士白子友受易，又事前將軍蕭望之受論語，皆能傳其業。

translations.literal: At age forty he then changed his course and studied the Changes with Erudite Bai Ziyou, and also studied the Analects with former General Xiao Wangzhi—both could transmit their learning.
translations.idiomatic: At forty he reformed, studied the Zhou yi under Doctor Bai Ziyou and the Analects under the retired general Xiao Wangzhi, and mastered both teachers.

sentences.zh: 好倜儻大節，〈師古曰：「倜音吐歷反。」

translations.literal: He loved grand integrity and unconstrained spirit. 〈Yan's note: "Ti is read tu-li fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: He admired heroic independence—Yan Shigu gives the fanqie for ti.

sentences.zh: 〉當世以是高之。

translations.literal: The age on this account esteemed him.
translations.idiomatic: The capital set great store by him.

sentences.zh: 元帝時，琅邪貢禹爲御史大夫，而華陰守丞嘉上封事，〈師古曰：「守華陰縣丞者，其人名嘉。」

translations.literal: Under Emperor Yuan, Gong Yu of Langya was Imperial Counselor, while the acting guardian of Huayin, chief clerk Jia, submitted a sealed memorial. 〈Yan's note: "He is the man named Jia who was acting chief clerk of Huayin county."〉
translations.idiomatic: Under Yuan, Imperial Counselor Gong Yu of Langya received a sealed memorial from Jia, acting county chief clerk of Huayin—Yan Shigu names the man Jia.

sentences.zh: 〉言『治道在於得賢，御史之官，宰相之副，九卿之右，〈師古曰：「右言在上也。」

translations.literal: 〉saying 'the way of good order lies in obtaining the worthy; the office of Imperial Counselor is the chancellor's deputy, to the right of the nine ministers—〈Yan's note: "You means above."〉
translations.idiomatic: It argued that order rests on worthy ministers, that the censor is the chancellor's right hand and outranks the nine ministers—Yan Shigu reads you as "senior."

sentences.zh: 〉不可不選。

translations.literal: it cannot but be carefully chosen.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial insisted that the post must be filled with care.

sentences.zh: 平陵朱雲，兼資文武，忠正有智略，可使以六百石秩試守御史大夫，以盡其能。」

translations.literal: Zhu Yun of Pingling combines civil and military talent, is loyal, upright, and has strategy; he may be tried as acting Imperial Counselor at six hundred piculs salary to exhaust his ability.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun of Pingling blends civil and martial gifts with loyal wit; try him as acting imperial counselor at six hundred piculs and see what he can do.

sentences.zh: 上迺下其事問公卿。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor sent the matter down to ask the dukes and ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor bounced the memorial to the high assembly.

sentences.zh: 太子少傅匡衡對，以爲「大臣者，國家之股肱，萬姓所瞻仰，明王所慎擇也。

translations.literal: Junior Tutor to the Heir Kuang Heng replied, holding that "great ministers are the state's thighs and arms, what the myriad surnames look up to, whom an enlightened king chooses with care.
translations.idiomatic: Junior tutor Kuang Heng answered that great ministers are the state's limbs, the people's mirror, and a wise king picks them with utmost care.

sentences.zh: 傳曰下輕其上爵，賤人圖柄臣，則國家搖動而民不靜矣。

translations.literal: The tradition says: if those below lightly esteem their superiors' ranks and base men plot against ministers who hold the handle, then the state trembles and the people are not quiet.
translations.idiomatic: The classics warn that when commoners despise high rank and plot against power-holders, the realm shakes.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「上爵，大官也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Shang jue means great offices.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: shang jue means exalted posts.

sentences.zh: 圖，謀也。

translations.literal: Tu means to plot.
translations.idiomatic: Tu is "to scheme."

sentences.zh: 柄臣，執權之臣。」

translations.literal: Bing chen means a minister who holds power."〉
translations.idiomatic: Bing chen is a minister who wields the levers of power.

sentences.zh: 〉今嘉從守丞而圖大臣之位，欲以匹夫徒步之人而超九卿之右，非所以重國家而尊社稷也。

translations.literal: Now Jia, starting from acting chief clerk, plots the place of a great minister, wishing with the body of a commoner on foot to leap above the nine ministers—it is not the way to give weight to the state and honor the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Jia, a mere acting clerk, would vault a foot soldier into the censor's chair—not how you steady the altars.

sentences.zh: 自堯之用舜，文王於太公，猶試然後爵之，又況朱雲者乎？

translations.literal: From Yao's employment of Shun down to King Wen's use of the Grand Duke, they still examined carefully and then enfeoffed them—how much the more in the case of Zhu Yun?
translations.idiomatic: Even Yao with Shun and King Wen with the Grand Duke tested them long before handing seals—would Zhu Yun be rushed faster?

sentences.zh: 雲素好勇，數犯法亡命，受易頗有師道，其行義未有以異。

translations.literal: Yun has always loved courage, repeatedly violated the law and fled for his life; in receiving the Changes he has some teacherly method, but in conduct and righteousness there is nothing to distinguish him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu loves a fight, has fled the law more than once, knows the Changes well enough to teach, yet shows nothing singular in conduct.

sentences.zh: 今御史大夫禹絜白廉正，經術通明，有伯夷、史魚之風，海內莫不聞知，而嘉猥稱雲，〈師古曰：「猥，曲也。」

translations.literal: Now Imperial Counselor Yu is pure, white, incorrupt, and upright, his classical learning clear and penetrating, with the air of Boyi and Ziyu of Wei—none within the seas fails to hear and know—yet Jia vulgarly praises Yun, 〈Yan's note: "Wei means 'crooked.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Gong Yu is stainless, learned, and as straight as Boyi or Ziyu—everyone knows it—yet Jia crookedly praises Zhu Yun. Yan Shigu: wei means "twisted" or "improper."

sentences.zh: 〉欲令爲御史大夫，妄相稱舉，疑有姦心，漸不可長，宜下有司案驗以明好惡。」

translations.literal: wishing to make him Imperial Counselor, recklessly recommending each other—I suspect a treacherous heart; such gradual encroachment must not be allowed to grow; it is fitting to send it down to the authorities to investigate and verify, to clarify good and evil.'
translations.idiomatic: He would make Zhu censor by mutual puffery—smelling conspiracy, Kuang asked for a full inquest before the rot spreads.

sentences.zh: 嘉竟坐之。

translations.literal: In the end Jia sat guilty for it.
translations.idiomatic: Jia paid for the stunt with his career.

sentences.zh: 是時，少府五鹿充宗貴幸，爲梁丘易。

translations.literal: At that time Junior Minister Wulu Chongzong was noble and favored, and lectured on the Liangqiu school's Changes.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Junior Minister Wulu Chongzong stood high at court expounding the Liangqiu interpretation of the Changes.

sentences.zh: 自宣帝時善梁丘氏說，元帝好之，欲考其異同，令充宗與諸易家論。

translations.literal: From Emperor Xuandi's time they had been good at the Liangqiu explanations; Emperor Yuan loved them and wished to examine their sameness and difference, ordering Chongzong to debate with the various Changes scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Since Xuandi the court had favored Liangqiu; Yuan ordered a showdown between Chongzong and every other school.

sentences.zh: 充宗乘貴辯口，〈師古曰：「乘，因也。

translations.literal: Chongzong rode on his nobility and a sharp tongue. 〈Yan's note: "Cheng means 'because of.'
translations.idiomatic: Chongzong traded on rank and a glib tongue—Yan Shigu glosses cheng as "ride on" or "trade on."

sentences.zh: 言因藉尊貴之權也。」

translations.literal: It means relying on the power of nobility and honor."〉
translations.idiomatic: He leaned on imperial favor to cow the doctors.

sentences.zh: 〉諸儒莫能與抗，皆稱疾不敢會。

translations.literal: The various scholars none could withstand him; all claimed illness and dared not meet.
translations.idiomatic: Every scholar feigned sickness rather than face him.

sentences.zh: 有薦雲者，召入，攝𪗋登堂，〈師古曰：「𪗋，衣下之裳，音子私反。」

translations.literal: Someone recommended Yun; he was summoned in, tucked his lower robe and ascended the hall. 〈Yan's note: "Zi is the lower skirt of the garment, read zi-si fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: Someone nominated Zhu Yun; he strode in, tucked his court skirt, and mounted the dais—Yan Shigu identifies zi as the lower robe, fanqie zi-si.

sentences.zh: 〉抗首而請，〈師古曰：「抗，舉也。」

translations.literal: 〉He raised his head and requested. 〈Yan's note: "Kang means 'to raise.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: He lifted his head to speak—Yan Shigu: kang is "raise."

sentences.zh: 〉音動左右。

translations.literal: His voice moved those left and right.
translations.idiomatic: His voice shook the hall.

sentences.zh: 旣論難，連拄五鹿君，〈師古曰：「拄，刺也，距也，音竹庾反。」

translations.literal: When the debate and cross-examination were finished, he repeatedly rebuffed Lord Wulu. 〈Yan's note: "Zhu means to stab, to block, read zhu-yu fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: In disputation he pinned Chongzong again and again—Yan Shigu glosses zhu as "gore" or "parry," fanqie zhu-yu.

sentences.zh: 〉故諸儒爲之語曰：「五鹿嶽嶽，朱雲折其角。」

translations.literal: Therefore the various scholars made a saying of it: 'Wulu stands horned tall; Zhu Yun breaks his horn.'
translations.idiomatic: The doctors sang: "Wulu locks horns high—Zhu Yun snaps his tip."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「嶽嶽，長角之貌。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yueyue is the appearance of long horns."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: yueyue paints tall branching horns.

sentences.zh: 〉繇是爲博士。

translations.literal: Thereupon he became an erudite.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor named him an erudite.

sentences.zh: 遷杜陵令，坐故縱亡命，會赦，舉方正，爲槐里令。

translations.literal: He was transferred to be magistrate of Duling; for intentionally releasing fugitives he sat guilty, but it met an amnesty; he was recommended as upright and square and became magistrate of Huaili.
translations.idiomatic: He moved to Duling magistrate, fell for letting fugitives slip, then was pardoned, recommended as upright, and sent to Huaili.

sentences.zh: 時中書令石顯用事，與充宗爲黨，百僚畏之。

translations.literal: At that time Palace Secretary Shi Xian held power; he formed a faction with Chongzong, and the hundred officials feared them.
translations.idiomatic: Palace secretary Shi Xian ran the inner court in league with Chongzong, and the bureaucracy trembled.

sentences.zh: 唯御史中丞陳咸年少抗節，不附顯等，而與雲相結。

translations.literal: Only Palace Secretary to the Imperial Counselor Chen Xian, though young, upheld integrity, did not attach to Xian and the rest, but formed ties with Yun.
translations.idiomatic: Only young Chen Xian, assistant censor, defied Shi Xian and befriended Zhu Yun.

sentences.zh: 雲數上疏，言丞相韋玄成容身保位，亡能往來，〈李竒曰：「不能有所前卻也。」

translations.literal: Yun repeatedly submitted memorials saying Chancellor Wei Xuancheng preserved his person and held his place, unable to advance or retreat. 〈Li Qi's note: "Unable to have any forward or backward movement."〉
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun memorialized that Chancellor Wei Xuancheng clung to his cushion, neither advancing policy nor yielding—Li Qi reads the phrase as "unable to move either way."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「周書君奭之篇稱周公曰：『惟文王尚克修和有夏，有若虢叔、閎夭、散宜生、泰顛、南宮括。』

translations.literal: Yan's note: "In the 'Jun Shi' chapter of the Zhou Documents the Duke of Zhou says: 'Only King Wen was able to cultivate harmony with the Xia; there were such as Guo Shu, Hong Yao, San Yisheng, Tai Dian, Nangong Kuo.'
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu quotes the "Jun Shi" chapter: the Duke of Zhou praises King Wen's ministers—Guo Shu, Hong Yao, San Yisheng, Tai Dian, and Nangong Kuo.

sentences.zh: 又曰『亡能往來』。

translations.literal: It also says 'unable to advance or retreat.'"
translations.idiomatic: The same passage speaks of men who could neither press forward nor step back."

sentences.zh: 故雲引此以爲言也。」

translations.literal: Therefore Yun cited this to speak."〉
translations.idiomatic: That is the line Zhu Yun invoked against Wei Xuancheng."

sentences.zh: 〉而咸數毀石顯。

translations.literal: Moreover Xian repeatedly slandered Shi Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xian meanwhile kept impeaching Shi Xian before the throne.

sentences.zh: 乆之，有司考雲，疑風吏殺人。

translations.literal: After a long while the authorities examined Yun, suspecting he had hinted clerks to kill people.
translations.idiomatic: Months later the authorities reopened Zhu Yun's file, suspecting he had urged underlings to kill.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「風讀曰諷。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Feng is read feng 'to hint.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads the character as feng, meaning to hint or suggest indirectly.

sentences.zh: 〉羣臣朝見，上問丞相以雲治行。

translations.literal: When the ministers met in court audience, the emperor asked the chancellor about Yun's conduct in governing.
translations.idiomatic: At the next great audience Emperor Yuan asked the chancellor how Zhu Yun had governed.

sentences.zh: 丞相玄成言雲暴虐亡狀。

translations.literal: Chancellor Xuancheng said Yun was cruel and violent without good aspect.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xuancheng replied that Zhu Yun was savage and had left no creditable record.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「無善狀也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "There was no good appearance."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses "no good aspect" as no praiseworthy conduct to report.

sentences.zh: 〉時陳咸在前，聞之，以語雲。

translations.literal: 〉At that time Chen Xian was in front; hearing it, he told Yun.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xian stood in the front ranks, overheard the answer, and warned Zhu Yun.

sentences.zh: 雲上書自訟，咸爲定奏草，求下御史中丞。

translations.literal: Yun submitted a memorial defending himself; Xian drafted the memorial for him, seeking to send it down to the Palace Secretary to the Imperial Counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun filed a memorial in his own defense; Chen Xian composed the draft and asked that it be referred to the assistant censor.

sentences.zh: 事下丞相，丞相部吏考立其殺人罪。

translations.literal: The affair was sent down to the chancellor; the chancellor's subordinate clerks examined and established his crime of murder.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor's yamen ran the inquest until clerks could enter a murder conviction in the record.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「立，成也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Li means to complete."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses li as "establish" or "complete."

sentences.zh: 〉雲亡入長安，復與咸計議。

translations.literal: 〉Yun fled into Chang'an and again plotted with Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun slipped into Chang'an and took counsel again with Chen Xian.

sentences.zh: 丞相具發其事，奏「咸宿衞執法之臣，幸得進見，漏泄所聞，以私語雲，爲定奏草，欲令自下治，〈師古曰：「咸爲御史中丞，而奏請下中丞，故云自下治。」

translations.literal: The chancellor fully exposed the affair, memorializing: "Xian is a minister who guards the palace and upholds the law; he had the fortune to gain audience, leaked what he heard, spoke privately to Yun, drafted his memorial, and wished to have the matter sent down to himself for trial. 〈Yan's note: "Xian was Palace Secretary to the Imperial Counselor yet asked that the case go down to the assistant censor—hence 'send down to himself for trial.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor laid out the whole intrigue: Chen Xian held a law officer's post, had heard state business in audience, leaked it to Zhu Yun, ghost-wrote his defense, and tried to route the case to his own bureau—Yan Shigu notes the irony of an assistant censor referring a case to himself.

sentences.zh: 〉後知雲亡命罪人，而與交通，雲以故不得。」

translations.literal: 〉Only afterward did he learn that Yun was a condemned fugitive, yet still trafficked with him—therefore Yun could not be taken."
translations.idiomatic: He knew Zhu was a wanted fugitive yet conspired with him anyway—so Zhu could not be seized."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「吏捕之不得。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "The clerks could not catch him."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: the officers could not run him to ground.

sentences.zh: 〉上於是下咸、雲獄，減死爲城旦。

translations.literal: 〉Thereupon the emperor cast Xian and Yun into prison, commuting execution to wall corvée.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor jailed both men and spared their lives for hard labor on the walls.

sentences.zh: 咸、雲遂廢錮，終元帝世。

translations.literal: Xian and Yun were then ruined and barred from office for the rest of Emperor Yuan's reign.
translations.idiomatic: Both were disgraced and blocked from employment until Yuan died.

sentences.zh: 至成帝時，丞相故安昌侯張禹以帝師位特進，甚尊重。

translations.literal: By Emperor Cheng's time the former Marquis of Anchang, Zhang Yu, as the emperor's teacher held rank as specially advanced and was greatly honored.
translations.idiomatic: Under Cheng, Zhang Yu of Anchang—once the emperor's tutor—sat as specially advanced minister and basked in deference.

sentences.zh: 雲上書求見，公卿在前。

translations.literal: Yun submitted a memorial asking for audience; the dukes and ministers stood before him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun demanded a hearing with the high ministers looking on.

sentences.zh: 雲曰：「今朝庭大臣上不能匡主，下亡以益民，皆尸位素餐，〈師古曰：「尸，主也。

translations.literal: Yun said: "The great ministers at court today neither correct the ruler above nor benefit the people below—they all hold a corpse's seat and eat empty salary. 〈Yan's note: "Shi means 'to preside.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun said the high ministers neither steadied the throne nor helped the people—they were "corpse seat" placeholders drawing empty pay. Yan Shigu glosses shi as "preside."

sentences.zh: 素，空也。

translations.literal: Su means empty.
translations.idiomatic: Su means hollow or wasted.

sentences.zh: 尸位者，不舉其事，但主其位而已。

translations.literal: One who holds a corpse's seat does not advance the business but only occupies the place.
translations.idiomatic: They warm the bench without doing the job.

sentences.zh: 素餐者，德不稱官，空當食祿。」

translations.literal: One who eats empty salary—his virtue does not match his office, yet he idly draws stipend."
translations.idiomatic: They draw pay while their virtue never matches their rank."

sentences.zh: 〉孔子所謂『鄙夫不可與事君』，『苟患失之，亡所不至』者也。

translations.literal: 〉They are what Confucius called "the base fellow with whom one cannot serve a lord"—"once he fears losing favor, there is nothing he will not do."
translations.idiomatic: They are the petty men Confucius warned against—clinging to place until they will commit any crime.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「皆論語所載孔子之言也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "These are all sayings of Confucius recorded in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: both quotations come from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 苟患失其寵祿，則言行僻邪，無所不至也。」

translations.literal: If he fears losing his favor and emolument, then his words and deeds turn crooked and there is nothing he will not reach to."
translations.idiomatic: Terror for salary twists every word and deed."

sentences.zh: 〉臣願賜尚方斬馬劔，斷佞臣一人以厲其餘。」

translations.literal: 〉Your servant begs to be granted the Palace Workshop's horse-beheading sword to strike off one fawning minister and warn the rest."
translations.idiomatic: Give me the imperial blade reserved for traitors—one stroke on a sycophant to sober the rest."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「尚方，少府之屬官也，作供御器物，故有斬馬劔，劔利可以斬馬也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Shangfang is a sub-bureau of the Junior Minister; it makes objects for imperial use, hence the horse-beheading sword—so sharp it can sever a horse."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: Shangfang forges the court's arms, including the ceremonial sword sharp enough to cleave a charger.

sentences.zh: 〉上問：「誰也？」

translations.literal: 〉The emperor asked, "Who?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor snapped, "Name him."

sentences.zh: 對曰「安昌侯張禹。」

translations.literal: He answered, "Marquis of Anchang Zhang Yu."
translations.idiomatic: "Zhang Yu of Anchang."

sentences.zh: 上大怒，曰：「小臣居下訕上，〈師古曰：「訕，謗也，音所諫反，又音刪。」

translations.literal: The emperor was greatly angered and said, "A petty clerk below slanders those above—〈Yan's note: "Shan means to revile; read suo-jian fan, also read shan."〉
translations.idiomatic: Cheng boiled over: "A low clerk defames his betters—Yan Shigu glosses shan as revile."

sentences.zh: 〉廷辱師傅，罪死不赦。」

translations.literal: 〉You have humiliated the tutor in open court—death without pardon."
translations.idiomatic: You have insulted my teacher in the hall—no pardon."

sentences.zh: 御史將雲下，雲攀殿檻，檻折。

translations.literal: The Imperial Clerk led Yun down; Yun seized the palace threshold; the threshold broke.
translations.idiomatic: Guards dragged Zhu away; he locked arms on the door sill until the carved rail snapped.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「檻，軒前欄也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Kan is the railing before the hall."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: kan is the hall railing.

sentences.zh: 〉雲呼曰：〈師古曰：「呼，叫也，音火故反。」

translations.literal: 〉Yun cried out—〈Yan's note: "Hu means to shout; read huo-gu fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: Zhu roared—Yan Shigu glosses hu as shout.

sentences.zh: 〉「臣得下從龍逢、比干遊於地下，足矣！

translations.literal: 〉"If your servant may follow Long Peng and Prince Bi Gan in the world below, I am content!
translations.idiomatic: "Let me join Long Peng and Bi Gan in the grave—I am content!

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「關龍逢，桀臣，王子比干，紂之諸父，皆以諫而死，故云然。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Guan Longfeng was Jie's minister; Prince Bi Gan was Zhou's uncle—both died remonstrating, hence he speaks so."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu names the two martyred remonstrators of Jie and Zhou.

sentences.zh: 〉未知聖朝何如耳？」

translations.literal: 〉I do not yet know what this sage court will be like!"
translations.idiomatic: What your sage dynasty will look like, I cannot yet say!"

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「言殺直臣其聲惡。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "It means killing straight ministers brings an ill name."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: executing honest critics stains the throne.

sentences.zh: 〉御史遂將雲去。

translations.literal: 〉Thereupon the clerk led Yun away.
translations.idiomatic: The guards marched him off.

sentences.zh: 於是左將軍辛慶忌免冠解印綬，叩頭殿下曰：「此臣素著狂直於世。

translations.literal: Then General of the Left Xin Qingji removed his cap, loosed his seals and ribbons, and knocked his head below the dais, saying, "This subject has long been known to the world for mad bluntness.
translations.idiomatic: Left general Xin Qingji tore off cap and sash and kowtowed: "The man is famous for wild integrity."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「著，表也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Zhu means to display."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: zhu means "display" or "show forth."

sentences.zh: 言此名乆彰表。」

translations.literal: It means this name has long been displayed abroad."〉
translations.idiomatic: His reputation has stood for years."

sentences.zh: 〉使其言是，不可誅；

translations.literal: 〉If his words are right, he must not be executed;
translations.idiomatic: If he speaks truth, spare him;

sentences.zh: 其言非，固當容之。

translations.literal: if his words are wrong, he should still be indulged.
translations.idiomatic: if he is wrong, still indulge his bluntness.

sentences.zh: 臣敢以死爭。」

translations.literal: Your servant dares contend for him even at the cost of death."
translations.idiomatic: I will plead for him on pain of death."

sentences.zh: 慶忌叩頭流血。

translations.literal: Qingji knocked his head until it bled.
translations.idiomatic: Xin battered his brow on the stone until blood ran.

sentences.zh: 上意解，然後得已。

translations.literal: The emperor's anger eased, and only then was the matter stopped.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng's rage cooled and the execution halted.

sentences.zh: 及後當治檻，上曰：「勿易！

translations.literal: Later, when they were about to repair the threshold, the emperor said, "Do not replace it!
translations.idiomatic: When carpenters came to mend the rail, the emperor said, "Leave it broken!

sentences.zh: 因而輯之，以旌直臣。」

translations.literal: Rather join it up as it is, to mark a straight-speaking minister."
translations.idiomatic: Patch the break and keep it as a monument to blunt loyalty."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「輯與集同，謂補合之也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Ji is the same as ji 'to gather'—meaning to mend and join."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads ji like ji, to patch together.

sentences.zh: 旌，表也。」〉

translations.literal: Jing means to display."〉
translations.idiomatic: Jing means to set forth for all to see."

sentences.zh: 雲自是之後不復仕，常居鄠田，時出乘牛車從諸生，所過皆敬事焉。

translations.literal: After this Yun never again took office; he constantly dwelt on his lands at Hu, sometimes riding out in an ox-cart among the scholars, and wherever he passed they treated him with respect.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun never served again. He farmed at Hu, sometimes toured in an ox-cart with students, and every village honored him.

sentences.zh: 薛宣爲丞相，雲往見之。

translations.literal: When Xue Xuan became chancellor, Yun went to see him.
translations.idiomatic: When Xue Xuan took the chancellorship, Zhu called on him.

sentences.zh: 宣備賔主禮，因留雲宿，從容謂雲曰：〈師古曰：「從音七庸反。」

translations.literal: Xuan prepared full host-and-guest courtesy, then detained Yun overnight and, at leisure, said to Yun—〈Yan's note: "Cong is read qi-yong fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: Xue laid a feast, kept him overnight, and over wine said—Yan Shigu gives fanqie for cong.

sentences.zh: 〉「在田野亡事，且留我東閤，可以觀四方竒士。」

translations.literal: 〉"You have nothing to do in the fields—stay awhile in my east pavilion and you may watch the unusual talents of the four quarters."
translations.idiomatic: "Idle in the country—lodge in my east wing and watch the empire's odd talents pass through."

sentences.zh: 雲曰：「小生迺欲相吏邪？」

translations.literal: Yun said, "Does a young sprout mean to make me his clerk?"
translations.idiomatic: "You fresh-faced stripling—you think you can press me into clerking for you?"

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「小生謂其新學後進。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Xiao sheng means a newly advanced student.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: xiao sheng is a junior scholar.

sentences.zh: 言欲以我爲吏乎？」

translations.literal: Does it mean you wish to use me as a clerk?"〉
translations.idiomatic: Are you trying to hire me as a secretary?"

sentences.zh: 〉宣不敢復言。

translations.literal: 〉Xuan dared speak no more.
translations.idiomatic: Xue never raised it again.

sentences.zh: 其敎授，擇諸生，然後爲弟子。

translations.literal: In his teaching he chose among the scholars and only then made disciples.
translations.idiomatic: He picked disciples only from the worthiest students.

sentences.zh: 九江嚴望及望兄子元，字仲，能傳雲學，皆爲博士。

translations.literal: Yan Wang of Jiujiang and Wang's nephew Yuan, courtesy Zhong, could transmit Yun's learning; both became erudites.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Wang of Jiujiang and his nephew Yan Yuan mastered Zhu's school; both rose to erudite.

sentences.zh: 望至泰山太守。

translations.literal: Wang rose to Guardian of Taishan.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Wang governed Taishan.

sentences.zh: 雲年七十餘，終於家。

translations.literal: Yun lived past seventy and ended his days at home.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun died at home past seventy.

sentences.zh: 病不呼醫飲藥。

translations.literal: When ill he did not summon physicians or take medicine.
translations.idiomatic: Sick, he refused doctors and drugs.

sentences.zh: 遺言以身服斂，棺周於身，土周於椁，〈師古曰：「棺周於身，小棺裁容身也。

translations.literal: He left instructions to be clothed for burial only to the body's measure, a coffin just fitting the frame, earth just enclosing the outer shell—〈Yan's note: "The coffin circles the body—a small coffin barely holding the frame.
translations.idiomatic: His will demanded a snug shroud, a coffin no wider than his shoulders, a pit barely wider than the shell—Yan Shigu explains the frugal measure.

sentences.zh: 土周於椁，冢壙裁容椁也。」

translations.literal: Earth circles the outer shell—the grave pit barely holds the shell."〉
translations.idiomatic: The shaft would only admit the inner box."

sentences.zh: 〉爲丈五墳，葬平陵東郭外。

translations.literal: 〉He asked for a mound one zhang five chi high, buried outside the eastern wall of Pingling.
translations.idiomatic: He asked for a burial mound one zhang and five chi high east of Pingling's outer wall.

sentences.zh: 梅福字子真，九江壽春人也。

translations.literal: Mei Fu, courtesy name Zizhen, was a man of Shouchun in Jiujiang.
translations.idiomatic: Mei Fu, courtesy Zizhen, came from Shouchun in Jiujiang.

sentences.zh: 少學長安，明尚書、穀梁春秋，爲郡文學，補南昌尉。

translations.literal: In youth he studied in Chang'an, mastering the Documents and the Guliang commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals; he became literary scholar of the commandery and was appointed constable of Nanchang.
translations.idiomatic: He studied in Chang'an, mastered the Shang shu and Guliang Annals, served as commandery instructor, then constable of Nanchang.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「豫章之縣。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "A county in Yuzhang."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: a Yuzhang county.

sentences.zh: 〉後去官歸壽春，數因縣道上言變事，〈師古曰：「附縣道之使而封奏也。

translations.literal: 〉Later he left office and returned to Shouchun, repeatedly using county couriers to memorialize on portents—〈Yan's note: "He attached sealed memorials to county couriers."
translations.idiomatic: Home in Shouchun he kept tipping off the throne through county post riders—Yan Shigu: he hitched memorials to official couriers.

sentences.zh: 變謂非常之事。」

translations.literal: Bian means extraordinary affairs."〉
translations.idiomatic: Bian means crises or anomalies."

sentences.zh: 〉求假軺傳，〈師古曰：「小車之傳也。

translations.literal: 〉He begged leave to borrow a light carriage's relay tallies—〈Yan's note: "The relay tallies of a light cart."
translations.idiomatic: He asked for relay tallies on a light wagon—Yan Shigu explains the small-carriage chop.

sentences.zh: 軺音遙。

translations.literal: Yao is read yao.
translations.idiomatic: Yao is read yao.

sentences.zh: 傳音張戀反。」

translations.literal: Zhuan is read zhang-lian fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan is read zhang-lian in fanqie."

sentences.zh: 〉詣行在所條對急政，〈師古曰：「條對者，一一條錄而對之。」

translations.literal: 〉to go to the imperial encampment and itemize answers on urgent policy—〈Yan's note: "Tiao dui means to list each item and answer."〉
translations.idiomatic: To reach the temporary palace and answer point by point on urgent reforms—Yan Shigu glosses tiao dui as itemized response.

sentences.zh: 〉輒報罷。

translations.literal: 〉Each time he was answered with dismissal.
translations.idiomatic: Each time the court brushed him off.

sentences.zh: 是時成帝委任大將軍王鳳，鳳專埶擅朝，而京兆尹王章素忠直，譏刺鳳，爲鳳所誅。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor Cheng entrusted affairs to Grand General Wang Feng; Feng monopolized power and ran the court, while the Governor of the Capital Wang Zhang, always loyal and blunt, satirized Feng and was executed by Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng handed power to Wang Feng; Feng choked the court. Wang Zhang of the capital spoke sharp truth and Feng had him killed.

sentences.zh: 王氏浸盛，〈師古曰：「浸，漸也。」

translations.literal: The Wang clan grew ever stronger—〈Yan's note: "Jin means gradually."〉
translations.idiomatic: The Wangs swelled—Yan Shigu: jin is "step by step."

sentences.zh: 〉災異數見，羣下莫敢正言。

translations.literal: 〉portents multiplied, yet none below dared speak straight.
translations.idiomatic: Omens stacked up while every tongue stayed tied.

sentences.zh: 福復上書曰：

translations.literal: Fu again submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Mei Fu memorialized again:

sentences.zh: 臣聞箕子佯狂於殷，而爲周陳洪範；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Jizi feigned madness in Yin yet laid out the Great Plan for Zhou;
translations.idiomatic: Jizi played the madman under Shang yet gave Zhou the Hong fan;

sentences.zh: 叔孫通遁秦歸漢，制作儀品。

translations.literal: Shusun Tong fled Qin and returned to Han, framing ritual and regulations.
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong slipped from Qin to Han and built the court's ritual code.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「遁，逃也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Dun means to flee."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: dun is flee.

sentences.zh: 〉夫叔孫先非不忠也，〈師古曰：「先猶言先生也。

translations.literal: 〉Now Shusun Tong was not disloyal—〈Yan's note: "Xian means sir or elder."
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong was no traitor—Yan Shigu glosses xian as "sir" or "venerable."

sentences.zh: 一曰，先謂在秦時。」

translations.literal: One opinion says xian refers to his time in Qin."〉
translations.idiomatic: Another gloss: xian means "while still in Qin."

sentences.zh: 〉箕子非疏其家而畔親也，〈師古曰：「箕子，紂之諸父，故言疏家畔親也。」

translations.literal: 〉Jizi did not cast off his kin to rebel against kin—〈Yan's note: "Jizi was Zhou's uncle, hence 'cast off kin to rebel against kin.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Jizi did not betray family to turn traitor—Yan Shigu reminds us he was the king's uncle.

sentences.zh: 〉不可爲言也。

translations.literal: 〉it was impossible to get a hearing.
translations.idiomatic: No one would listen.

sentences.zh: 昔高祖納善若不及，從諫若轉圜，〈師古曰：「不及，恐失之也。

translations.literal: Formerly Gaozu welcomed good counsel as if he could never catch up, followed remonstrance as easily as turning a round object—〈Yan's note: "Bu ji means fearing to miss it.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu drank in advice for fear of missing a word and spun on a remonstrance like a ball—Yan Shigu: bu ji is dread of losing counsel.

sentences.zh: 轉圜，言其順也。」

translations.literal: Zhuan yuan means compliant."〉
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan yuan means turning a round weight—easy motion, ready compliance."

sentences.zh: 〉聽言不求其能，舉功不考其素。

translations.literal: 〉He listened to words without demanding talent, rewarded deeds without investigating origins.
translations.idiomatic: He heard men out without demanding polish and rewarded deeds without probing pedigrees.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「直取其功，不論其舊行及所從來也。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "He took the merit alone, not debating old conduct or where they came from."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: take the deed at face value, not the résumé.

sentences.zh: 〉陳平起於亡命而爲謀主，韓信拔於行陳而建上將。

translations.literal: 〉Chen Ping rose from fugitive to become chief planner; Han Xin was pulled from the ranks and made supreme general.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping rose from exile as strategist; Han Xin leaped from the ranks to grand marshal.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「立以爲大將軍。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "He was appointed grand general."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: named grand general of the armies.

sentences.zh: 〉故天下之士雲合歸漢，〈師古曰：「言四面而至。」

translations.literal: 〉Therefore knights of the empire flocked to Han like clouds—〈Yan's note: "It means they came from all sides."〉
translations.idiomatic: Talent poured to Han from every quarter—Yan Shigu: like clouds converging.

sentences.zh: 〉爭進竒異，知者竭其策，愚者盡其慮，勇士極其節，怯夫勉其死。

translations.literal: 〉They vied to offer strange plans: the wise exhausted their schemes, the foolish poured out their anxieties, the brave spent their utmost integrity, the coward steeled himself to face death.
translations.idiomatic: Wise men laid schemes, fools found courage, heroes spent their nerve, cowards learned to die.

sentences.zh: 合天下之知，并天下之威，是以舉秦如鴻毛，取楚若拾遺，〈師古曰：「鴻毛喻輕。

translations.literal: They pooled the wit of the realm and the majesty of the realm—therefore they lifted Qin as lightly as a goose feather and took Chu like picking up lost goods—〈Yan's note: "Goose feather means light.
translations.idiomatic: All minds and all awe united—Qin fell like fluff, Chu like snatching dropped coins—Yan Shigu: goose feather for lightness.

sentences.zh: 拾遺，言其易也。」

translations.literal: Shi yi means easy."〉
translations.idiomatic: Shi yi means as easy as stooping for a trifle."

sentences.zh: 〉此高祖所以亡敵於天下也。

translations.literal: 〉This is how Gaozu had no match under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: That is how Gaozu became unbeatable.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「亡讀曰無。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Wu is read the same as wu 'without.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads wu like wu, "without peer."

sentences.zh: 〉孝文皇帝起於代谷，〈師古曰：「從代而來即帝位。」

translations.literal: 〉Emperor Wen rose from the Dai valley—〈Yan's note: "He came from Dai to take the throne."〉
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen came from the Dai commandery to the throne—Yan Shigu notes his path.

sentences.zh: 〉非有周召之師，伊呂之佐也，〈師古曰：「召讀曰邵。」

translations.literal: 〉without the tutors of Zhou and Shao or the assistants of Yi and Lu—〈Yan's note: "Shao is read shao."〉
translations.idiomatic: Without the Zhou-Shao tutors or Yi-Lu ministers—Yan Shigu: Shao reads shao.

sentences.zh: 〉循高祖之法，加以恭儉。

translations.literal: 〉he followed Gaozu's laws and added reverence and frugality.
translations.idiomatic: He kept Gaozu's code and layered on modest thrift.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，天下幾平。

translations.literal: At that time the realm was almost at peace.
translations.idiomatic: The realm neared peace.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「幾音距依反。」

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Ji is read ju-yi fan."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: ji is read ju-yi.

sentences.zh: 〉繇是言之，循高祖之法則治，不循則亂。

translations.literal: 〉From this speaking, following Gaozu's laws brings order; not following brings chaos.
translations.idiomatic: Follow Gaozu's pattern and the land rests; abandon it and the land boils.

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 秦爲亡道，削仲尼之迹，滅周公之軌，〈師古曰：「軌，法也。」

translations.literal: Qin practiced lawless ways, pared away Confucius's tracks, destroyed the Duke of Zhou's norms—〈Yan's note: "Gui means model."〉
translations.idiomatic: Qin hacked away Confucius, smashed the Duke of Zhou's models—Yan Shigu: gui is "pattern."

sentences.zh: 〉壞井田，除五等，禮廢樂崩，王道不通，故欲行王道者莫能致其功也。

translations.literal: 〉destroyed well-field grids, abolished the five ranks, ritual collapsed and music shattered, the kingly way was blocked—therefore whoever wished to enact the kingly way could not achieve its merit.
translations.idiomatic: It tore up well-fields and the five ranks until rites and music died and the royal way clogged—so no later king could finish the work.

sentences.zh: 孝武皇帝好忠諫，說至言，〈師古曰：「說讀曰悅。」

translations.literal: Emperor Wu loved loyal remonstrance and delighted in utmost words—〈Yan's note: "Yue is read yue 'delight.'"〉
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu welcomed blunt counsel and prized straight speech—Yan Shigu reads yue as delight.

sentences.zh: 〉出爵不待廉茂，慶賜不須顯功，〈師古曰：「謂諫爭合意即得官爵，不由薦舉及軍功也。

translations.literal: 〉he issued noble rank without waiting for "incorrupt" or "abundant talent," granted rewards without requiring manifest merit—〈Yan's note: "It means if remonstrance pleased him he gave office and rank without recommendation or military merit.
translations.idiomatic: He handed out fiefs when a memorial pleased him—no need for the usual incorrupt or abundant-talent nominations or battle laurels.

sentences.zh: 廉，廉吏也。

translations.literal: Lian means an incorrupt official.
translations.idiomatic: Lian names the "incorrupt" recommendation class.

sentences.zh: 茂，茂材也。」

translations.literal: Mao means abundant talent."〉
translations.idiomatic: Mao names the "abundant talent" class."

sentences.zh: 〉是以天下布衣各厲志竭精以赴闕廷自衒鬻者不可勝數。

translations.literal: 〉Therefore every commoner in the realm sharpened his will and spent his wit to hurry to the palace gate to offer himself—beyond counting.
translations.idiomatic: Commoners sharpened wits and thronged the capital to sell their service—uncountable.

sentences.zh: 漢家得賢，於此爲盛。

translations.literal: In this the Han house gained worthy men at its fullest tide.
translations.idiomatic: Han talent peaked in that age.

sentences.zh: 使孝武皇帝聽用其計，升平可致。

translations.literal: Had Emperor Wu listened to their plans, the age of general peace could have been reached.
translations.idiomatic: Had Wu used their advice, the realm might have reached true peace.

sentences.zh: 〈張晏曰：「民有三年之儲曰升平。」

translations.literal: 〈Zhang Yan's note: "When the people hold three years' stores it is called shengping."〉
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Yan defines shengping as years when granaries hold three years of grain.

sentences.zh: 〉於是積尸暴骨，快心胡越，故淮南王安緣間而起。

translations.literal: 〉Then corpses were piled and bones exposed while hearts rejoiced over the Hu and Yue—therefore the king of Huainan, An, seized the gap and rose.
translations.idiomatic: Instead came slaughter abroad and pride at crushing barbarians—so Liu An of Huainan saw his opening.

sentences.zh: 所以計慮不成而謀議泄者，以衆賢聚於本朝，〈師古曰：「本朝，漢朝也。」

translations.literal: The reason his plots never matured and his counsels leaked was that many worthies crowded this court—〈Yan's note: "Ben chao means the Han court."〉
translations.idiomatic: Why the plot failed: talent still packed the Han capital—Yan Shigu glosses ben chao as our dynasty.

sentences.zh: 〉故其大臣埶陵不敢和從也。

translations.literal: 〉therefore his great ministers' power was checked and they dared not join in assent.
translations.idiomatic: Huainan's ministers could not bully the ruler into conspiracy.

sentences.zh: 〈服虔曰：「臣勢陵君也。」

translations.literal: 〈Fu Qian's note: "The ministers' power overawed the ruler."〉
translations.idiomatic: Fu Qian reads it as ministers dominating their lord.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「謂淮南大臣相內史之屬也。」

translations.literal: Yan's note: "It means Huainan's ministers such as the chancellor and palace secretary."〉
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: Huainan's inner circle of high officials.

sentences.zh: 〉方今布衣迺窺國家之隙，見間而起者，蜀郡是也。

translations.literal: 〉Today commoners spy out cracks in the state; those who rise when they see a gap—Shu commandery is the case.
translations.idiomatic: Today rustics watch for weakness—Shu rose first.

sentences.zh: 〈孟康曰：「成帝鴻嘉中廣漢男子鄭躬等反是也。」

translations.literal: 〈Meng Kang's note: "It means the Guanghan men Zheng Gong and others who rebelled in the Hongjia era of Cheng."〉
translations.idiomatic: Meng Kang cites Zheng Gong's Guanghan rising under Cheng's Hongjia.

sentences.zh: 〉及山陽亡徒蘇令之羣，蹈藉名都大郡，求黨與，索隨和，〈李竒曰：「求索與己和及隨己者。」

translations.literal: 〉And the band of Shanyang fugitive Su Ling, trampling famous capitals and great commanderies, seeking allies, hunting for those who would follow or join them—〈Li Qi's note: "They sought men who would join them or go along with them."〉
translations.idiomatic: Then Su Ling's Shanyang band overran great cities, recruiting allies—Li Qi glosses sui he as followers.

sentences.zh: 〉而亡逃匿之意。

translations.literal: 〉yet they showed no thought of fleeing into hiding.
translations.idiomatic: They marched openly, never slinking into hiding.

sentences.zh: 此皆輕量大臣，亡所畏忌，國家之權輕，故匹夫欲與上爭衡也。

translations.literal: This all shows they hold the great ministers light, fear nothing, and the state's authority weighs little—so a commoner would match strength with the throne.
translations.idiomatic: They despise high ministers because the court's awe has thinned—commoners dare face the Son of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 士者，國之重器；

translations.literal: Knights are the state's heavy vessels;
translations.idiomatic: Talent is the dynasty's heavy armor;

sentences.zh: 得士則重，失士則輕。

translations.literal: gain knights and you are heavy, lose them and you are light.
translations.idiomatic: with them you stand firm, without them you totter.

sentences.zh: 詩云：「濟濟多士，文王以寧。」

translations.literal: The Odes says, "In hosts so many the knights were—King Wen thereby had peace."
translations.idiomatic: The Classic says many good men made King Wen secure.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「大雅文王之詩也。

translations.literal: 〈Yan's note: "It is a poem in the Greater Ya on King Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: from the Greater Ya "Wen Wang" ode.

sentences.zh: 已解於上。」

translations.literal: Already glossed above."〉
translations.idiomatic: As explained earlier."

sentences.zh: 〉廟堂之議，非草茅所當言也。

translations.literal: 〉Deliberations of the temple hall are not what thatch cottages should speak of.
translations.idiomatic: Inner-court policy is no topic for grass huts.
